data.title: UP Fintech: Turning Bullish On Q3 Growth Acceleration And Positive Full-Year Prospects (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I've revised UP Fintech's (TIGR) rating from 'Hold' to 'Buy'.

TIGR achieved a faster pace of growth and margin expansion in Q3. Its full-year results should be excellent as well, taking into account a number of positive factors.

The mixed findings from its 2Q25 disclosures were detailed in my previous August 28, 2025 write-up.

Latest Quarter Saw Topline Acceleration And Profitability Enhancement

TIGR's 3Q25 financials were released in a 6-K published on Thursday, Dec 4.

Its Jul-Sep '25 turnover was +73% higher at $0.18B on YoY terms. That was superior as compared to the group's 2Q2025 and 3Q2024 growth rates of +59% and +44%, respectively.

In my opinion, a vibrant trading environment was the main driver of TIGR's outperformance.

There's naturally increased speculation in micro-caps and "penny shares" when the market heats up. TIGR's execution fees are tied to the quantity of shares transacted. This explains why its 3Q2025 "take rate" for stocks rose 0.7bps (basis points) year-on-year to 0.071ppts (percentage points).

Investors are more likely to purchase shares with borrowed monies, as their risk appetites get bigger. Consequently, the company's interest income expanded by +53% YoY and +25% quarter-on-quarter to $73M for the 3rd quarter of 2025.

Corporates are also rushing for IPOs to capitalize on improved market sentiment. That led to a +189% surge in TIGR's "other revenues" to 3Q25's $26M. It was the underwriter for ten U.S./HK public listings during this period.

Separately, the firm's normalized net margin widened by +13ppts YoY and +1ppts sequentially to 33% in the recent three-month period. This also meant that its net profit went from 3Q24's $20M to 3Q2025's $57M.

The positive effects of fixed-cost leverage and an optimal change in user mix boosted profitability.

TIGR's Q3 media release emphasized that it has been "earning stronger trust from high-quality clients." It also revealed that "client assets in these two markets (Singapore and Hong Kong) grew approximately 20% and 60% QoQ (in 3Q25 respectively), highlighting a steady improvement in overall user quality."

Also, there are negligible incremental expenses incurred to serve additional customers with its tech-focused self-service trading platform. This translates into scale economies and higher margins for the group.

Its shares had gone up 4% on the day of results announcement. The investing community was clearly impressed with TIGR's Q3 showing. I take the same view too.

Anticipating Solid FY25 Numbers

TIGR is expected to report its full-year results in March next year.

The sell-side is predicting a +93% jump in the company's non-GAAP EPS to FY2025's $0.82 as per S&P Capital IQ. Its bottom-line growth in the preceding year was a relatively slower +57%.

I'm optimistic about TIGR's prospects.

A thing to watch is the firm's good performance in Q4 thus far.

At the analyst briefing, TIGR shared that "our trading volume for the first 2 months of the fourth quarter is already on par with the entire Q3." Its "net asset inflow quarter-to-date remains robust, and are expected to be slightly better than the Q3" according to management's call remarks.

The above-mentioned trends suggest that its positive momentum from 3Q will most probably be sustainable.

Also, TIGR's "Hong Kong/HK" business has reached an inflection point. The recent launch of fresh offerings in that market such as derivatives for Japan indices was well-received.

My early-Jun article noted that "the group is making headway in Hong Kong", which should lead to "financial improvement." The write-up also referred to its Singapore unit as the "flagship."

TIGR disclosed in its 6-K filing that "Singapore and Hong Kong" were "each accounting for roughly 40% of (3Q2025) new funded clients." The same metric for HK was 30% in 2Q25.

It's encouraging to see the HK division closing the gap with the Singapore counterpart. The former was only started three years ago, unlike the latter's operations which were established since 2020.

Also, TIGR's earnings are benefiting from favorable regulatory development.

Earlier in the year, SEC confirmed that "starting on May 14, 2025, the fee rates applicable to most securities transactions will be set at $0.00 per million dollars."

TIGR outlined the new rule's impact at its Q3 call. It indicated that "clearing costs decreased by 17% this quarter, bringing the quarterly clearing costs to a historic low of 6%." The company also guided that "the current clearing cost rate (at six percent) is quite sustainable as we are self-clearing for all core products."

My view is that it should do as well as, if not better than what the consensus is forecasting.

You Should Consider These Risks

TIGR's future financial metrics might fall short of expectations in the event that investors go into "risk-off" mode. This can happen because of geopolitical crises or economic downturns in the U.S. or China.

Its progress in HK could be derailed, if competing brokers put up a tougher fight. The rivals may introduce new products or roll out more promotions to stem the loss of market share to TIGR.

My Conclusion

I'm a bull on the name now.

According to S&P Capital IQ data, TIGR trades at 10.6 times FY26 P/E. The same valuation ratio for Futu Holdings (FUTU) 15.1x.

The stock's potential upside is +42% (15.1/10.6), using its listed comparable as a benchmark.

I believe a similarly outstanding full-year FY25 performance can justify a more demanding multiple for TIGR.

data.title: Monster Beverage: Buy This Global Energy Drink Champion With More Room To Run
data.description:
Thesis

Monster Beverage (NASDAQ:MNST) appears to be in a new cycle of profitability and global expansion that, in my opinion, is not yet fully recognized by the market.

Management presents a combination of aggressive commercial strategy, technological upgrade in the supply chain operation and a deep understanding of the needs of the energy drinks category.

Q32025 was impressive, with sales growth and more than doubling of earnings.

The Company is strengthening its brand without sacrificing margins while increasing its presence in critical markets outside the US.

Monster has become an uncommon story, combining global scale, low production costs, product innovation and a loyal customer base.

Therefore, according to my perspective the stock justifies a premium valuation and maintains substantial upside.

Financials

Monster announced Q32025 sales growth of nearly 17%, while net income was up 41%.

International sales growth was impressive +23%, bringing the international contribution to 43% of total revenue, the highest in the company’s history.

The 250 basis point expansion in gross margins was driven by a better product mix, higher prices and more efficient manufacturing.

In my opinion, this trend is sustainable, especially as the portfolio shifts toward Zero Sugar and Ultra products, which have higher margins.

The U.S. market continued to strengthen with double-digit growth, with the Ultra brand posting a 29%+ increase.

Operating income increased nearly 41%, suggesting that the company is keeping its costs under control.

With specific price increases starting November 1, i expect Q12026 to show further margin improvement.

As i see it, Monster has two more years of strong demand and high operating leverage ahead of it.

Corporate Developments & Strategic Outlook

The company’s profile now goes beyond the narrow definition of “energy drink company.”

It is essentially transforming into a multi-level player with a global reach.

In my personal assessment, the most significant developments that are changing Monster’s strategic trajectory are three.

First, the Company is strengthening its foundation in innovation.

2026 has already been announced as one of the richest years of new products in its history, with new proposals Ultra, Juice Monster, Monster Electric Blue, while also introducing a new line exclusively for women, under the FLRT brand.

It seems to me that this move has strong strategic value, as women remain the most underserved segment of the category and could significantly accelerate overall penetration.

Second, Monster is expanding its presence in EMEA, APAC and Latin America. The EMEA region ran at a pace of over 30%, with new markets recording leading positions for the first time.

Monster became the No. 1 brand in Greece and strengthened its position in the United Kingdom, Poland, Spain, Australia and the Middle East.

In China, the increase exceeded 40%, while in India it reached 54%.

I feel that these numbers show that Monster has not been saturated, but is in an early phase in the most populous markets in the world.

Third, the technological upgrade of the supply chain is a quiet but extremely important advantage.

The company is going full digital through SAP IBP, strengthening forecasting, production and logistics.

At the same time, it is creating new flavor houses in Brazil and China, reducing costs and distribution times.

My view is that this change will enhance efficiency in the coming years and will allow the company to launch more products with less risk of production bottlenecks.

Monster is also strengthening its presence in the alcoholic RTD category through Beast, Blind Lemon and the new Stunt Double.

This market can develop into a second pillar of growth within the next five years.

Valuation

The stock is currently trading at around $74, with a P/E of around 40x.For a company that has been growing at a steady double-digit rate and increasing margins this premium is not extreme.

The stock is trading near $74 (with a P/E of 40x) and for a Company that is growing at a steady double digit and is increasing its margins this premium looks reasonable.

In the conservative scenario, Monster returns to single-digit growth and to a P/E of 30x. At that level, the stock would decrease to $55–$58. I only see this scenario in case of a serious slowdown.

In the base case scenario, the Company keeps double digit earnings growth and stable margins, with P/E near 40x.

Under this scenario, the stock would move to $80 to $85 during 2026 as earnings continue to strengthen. I consider this the most realistic outlook.In the bullish scenario, international markets gain speed, the Ultra series expands its share and Monster achieves stronger operating leverage.

P/E approaches 45x or even 50x and the stock could trade between $95-$100.In my estimation this outcome is feasible as emerging markets are driving most of the company’s momentum.

The stock is +36% this year, which may look “moderate” at first but it has developed through a solid uptrend and brief, well controlled pullbacks.

Risks

Despite Monster's strong position there are risks that need to be considered.First, the energy drinks category is highly competitive.

Companies like Red Bull or newer players can put pressure to the company with aggressive offers and heavy advertising.

As far as i am concerned, Monster maintains a lead due to the breadth of its portfolio but the pressure in the US remains.

In addition, Monster is heavily dependent on the agreements with the Coca-Cola network for distribution.

Any malfunction or restructuring of the agreements could affect its own profitability. I think that the partnership is solid but still remains a structural risk.

The geographical factor is also a challenge. Emerging markets offer great prospects but also carry higher volatility in terms of currency, regulatory changes and taxation. The recent increase in the excise tax in the Mexican market is a typical example.

Similarly, markets such as Argentina show fluctuations that affect pricing and revenue.

Finally, Monster is moving more aggressively into categories like RTDs and wellness drinks and these markets have thinner margins and intense local competition and shifting resources toward them may create added execution risk.

Conclusion

Monster is one of the most well constructed companies in the FMCG industry and still has substantial growth potential ahead of it.

Management's strategy is clear, aggressive and deeply focused on global expansion.

The energy drinks category continues to grow, while Monster shows that it can gain share in almost all geographic markets.

High margins, good pricing strategy and a strong international presence supports my view that the stock has room for growth in the base and bullish scenarios.

Monster is a clear "BUY" for me.

data.title: Meta's Next Idea Could Be Bigger Than The Smartphone
data.description:
Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) stock has declined roughly 11% since its October highs, which to me, looks less like a warning and more like a buying opportunity. Sure, investors were spooked by swelling AI-related CapEx, but Meta is funding this buildout from a position of strength, with a cash-rich balance sheet and an ad machine that is already monetizing AI at scale. Advantage+ is quietly becoming a growth engine with both pricing and impressions rising together, a combo that can compound revenue faster than the headline numbers suggest.

The bigger story, though, is what comes next. Meta is not just upgrading ad targeting, it’s laying the groundwork for a platform shift. If products like its Ray-Ban Meta glasses keep scaling, they could move Meta from being “a collection of apps on your phone” to a “computer on your face” company. If Meta gets it right, it could unlock an entirely new vertical and hardware-driven ecosystem.

In this article, I break down why the market may be missing the point of Meta’s AI spend, what the key catalysts are, and why this dip could be the setup for the next leg higher.

Spoiler: My Strong Buy rating on Meta stock is unchanged.

Stock summary

Meta stock trades at around $661 per share, down about 11% since the end of Oct. This year, the stock has gained about 13% and roughly 8% over the past 12 months. Over a longer time period, Meta has grown by around 137% over the past 5 years.

Valuation-wise, Meta trades at a FWD P/E ratio of 27.5x on a GAAP basis. For starters, this metric tells us how much investors are willing to pay for every dollar of Meta’s annualized earnings. In this case, investors are paying $27.50 per $1 of earnings. This puts Meta at a 63% premium compared to the sector median.

That said, I believe this premium is warranted given Meta’s business model. I mean, it’s one of the most reliable, mega-cap tech companies of this decade. However, Meta stock has experienced a setback recently.

Why did Meta’s share prices decline?

Meta stock started to decline in late Oct. following the company’s third-quarter earnings report. And by the looks of it, the absolute biggest driver of this drop is the significant increase in AI spending plans. Investors were spooked.

In its third-quarter earnings report, Meta raised 2025 CapEx guidance to between $70 and $72 billion- up from a previous range of $66 - $72 billion. These are the funds primarily allocated for AI-related expenses like data centers and other essential equipment. On top of that, the company announced that CapEx in 2026 would be notably larger and that total expenses would grow significantly faster than in 2025. That also spooked investors, especially those who worried about margins being squeezed.

This relatively bearish sentiment was reinforced by uncertainties that the AI sector’s spending would actually pay dividends. Market pundit, Michael Burry even raised doubts about the actual AI accounting, citing concerns that hyperscales might’ve boosted their earnings by stretching out the assumed lifespan of their infrastructure assets.

On that note, other reports suggest that Peter Thiel’s firm and Softbank (OTCPK:SFTBY) sold their Nvidia positions, which created a secular headwind for tech companies pouring heavily into AI developments, and Meta Platforms is sure one of them.

But what’s perplexing is that Meta’s third-quarter results were actually really strong. Revenue rose 26% to $51.24 billion compared to the same period last year, which also outpaced market estimates by 3.71%. Although net income on a GAAP basis declined about 83% to $2.71 billion, $1.08 a share (basic), this was driven by a one-time non-cash income tax charge of $15.93 billion due to the newly enacted One Big Beautiful Bill Act. This one-time charge also works in Meta’s favor, as it will significantly reduce U.S. federal tax payments moving forward.

According to CFO Susan Li, without this one-time hit, net income and EPS would have landed at $18.6 billion $7.25 per share, respectively.

Moreover, GAAP gross margin is 82.03%, which is quite impressive, while operating income rose 18.35% to $20.53 billion. So overall, Meta’s financial performance is solid as usual.

Going back to the CapEx factor, which I believe is the biggest driver of this bearish sentiment. I think the market is missing the bigger picture. Meta is making these investments from a position of strength, not weakness. As you might be aware, the company has a substantial free cash flow of $10.62 billion, and cash and cash equivalents of $44.45 billion against a minimal long-term debt of just around $28.8 million.

Unlike many tech and AI companies, Meta is capable of investing for growth and staying financially healthy simultaneously, which is why I’m not overly concerned about CapEx.

What could drive Meta even higher?

Buying the dip right now would be justifiable, given the potential growth drivers down the line. Starting with the most immediate catalyst:

The ad business has improved with AI

Looking at the third quarter report, Meta’s AI investments are translating into revenue growth. Again, revenue rose 26% YOY, which is also Meta’s highest revenue growth rate since the first quarter of 2024, all thanks to its AI systems creating monetization opportunities from its user base.

Specifically, Meta’s ARR for end-to-end AI-powered ad solutions surpassed $60 billion. Putting it in perspective, this is what the company would make over a full year if current performance continues, and that $60 billion annually comes from AI ad tools alone- specifically the Advantage+ suite, excluding the rest of the business.

The Advantage+ suite is a set of tools that automates advertising campaigns for businesses, and over 4 million advertisers are now using it. When you look deeper, this AI integration has driven roughly 5% more ad conversions on Instagram and 3% on Facebook, which also serves as proof that Meta’s AI is making ads more effective. As a result, advertisers are willing to spend more as they get better results. Can you blame them?

Now, if we go down a level deeper, these improvements are Meta’s GEM, or Generative Ads Recommendation Model, and Andromeda, which are both advanced AI systems that Meta developed. Andromeda, in particular, is quite interesting. It’s essentially Meta’s architecture for ad targeting and was developed in partnership with Nvidia (NVDA).

Industry reports suggest that Andromeda is 10,000x more powerful than Meta’s old systems, though that might be a little overstated. In practice, every time we scroll through our newsfeeds on Facebook or Instagram, there are tens of millions of possible ads that Meta could show us. Andromeda’s role is to narrow that down to a few, most suitable options in milliseconds, and find what’s best for our interests based on our activities online.

About a year ago, Meta reported about Andromeda’s 6% recall improvement, which measures how accurately the system finds relevant ads, and also delivered an 8% improvement in ad quality on certain segments. The company also reported advertisers experienced a 22% increase in return on ad spend (ROAS). I suppose these figures are much better-looking today.

Going back to the present, Instagram reels have also become more centralized on the app with a substantial ARR. According to CEO Mark Zuckerberg:

Video is a particular bright spot with video time spent on Instagram up more than 30% since last year. And as video continues to grow across our apps, Reels now has an annual run rate of over $50 billion. Improvements in our recommendation systems will also become even more leveraged as the volume of AI-created content grows.

Instagram Reels is Meta’s answer to TikTok, and the fact that it’s generating $50 billion annually proves that the company can adapt as user behavior shifts.

As I went deeper into the earnings call, I spotted that Meta’s ad business benefited from two things happening at once. Specifically, average ad prices increased 10% compared to the same period last year, while impressions rose 14% over the same period. When both of these go up together, it creates a compounding effect where revenue grows faster than either metric alone.

Bottom line: Meta has industrialized AI at scale while most competitors are still figuring it out.

Meta’s Ray-Ban glasses might be the next computing platform

As you might’ve read somewhere else, Meta’s smart glasses are seeing significant growth. Specifically, sales were up more than 200% in the first half of 2025, and that signals an entirely new hardware revenue stream. The company that makes these glasses is EssilorLuxottica, and has shipped over 3.5 million pairs of Meta’s smart glasses through mid-2025.

Personally, I see this as more than Meta selling new gadgets, such as what Apple (AAPL) does. If this project goes well, Meta might own the next computing platform. Some might go as far as to argue that Apple and Alphabet (GOOG/GOOGL) launched something similar in the past, like the Apple Vision Pro and Google Glass, only top flops.

Well, aesthetics-wise, these glasses make you look like a cyborg or a diver with snorkeling gear, while Meta’s smart glasses are as minimal as they can be. This is likely why it gained more traction than the early birds of the segment. Now, remember how we went from computers to smartphones? These smart glasses have the potential to be the next major shift in how we interact with tech every day, though it might still take a while until we’re all wearing them.

Not convinced? IDC forecasts sales growth of global wearables to increase from 9.4 million units in 2025 to nearly 20 million in 2029. And according to IDC’s available data, Meta already holds over 70% of the global AR and VR market, so if it continues to execute, it can capture a considerable portion of that projected sales.

And consider that with a lower price point (compared with a cell phone) and >8 billion people on the planet, it's not so difficult to imagine a world where these types of glasses become the default way to stay productive and communicate with the world.

My take

So, what’s my take?

The market has given us a buying opportunity amid risks in overspending. Would I have liked to buy at the $590 levels that we saw a couple of weeks ago? Sure. But I did need some time to digest the news. What I found was that it was the company-specific and the sector-wide concerns about AI spending likely hammered the stock- to some degree. And make no mistake, Meta’s business foundation remains solid. In fact, the numbers mentioned above support it.

That said, Meta is building multiple durable revenue streams that should compound for years. But the real upside, though, is optionality: if Ray-Ban Meta glasses continue to scale, Meta could be in the midst of creating their very own “iPhone moment,” not through a phone, but through a new interface that we wear. In my view, this dip is less a warning sign and more a setup.

This, and for all the reasons stated above, my Strong Buy rating for META remains unchanged.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Low-Fire, Low-Hire Job Market, After Massive Overhiring By Big Tech And Others In 2021 And 2022
data.description:
But the initial unemployment insurance claims were a doozie that'll reverse next week.

Initial applications for unemployment benefits (“initial claims”) in the week through Saturday plunged from already low levels, in an outlier move, by 27,000 from the prior week to 191,000, seasonally adjusted, the lowest since 2022 (blue line in the chart).

These are applications for unemployment insurance that freshly laid-off people filed at state unemployment agencies, which then reported them to the US Department of Labor by the weekly deadline, which then combined the data and published it on Thursday.

Obviously, one or more big states didn’t get their claims data filed before deadline, this having been the week of Thanksgiving, which is what caused this plunge in claims. But that data gets picked up in the next week, and so we’ll see a spike in claims, which will undo part or all of Thursday’s plunge.

The four-week average fell to 214,750, seasonally adjusted, which is historically low and in the same low range that it has been in for the past four years.

The four-week average irons out the week-to-week squiggles of weeks when deadlines were missed and weeks when the missed data was included (red line in the chart).

In a historical context going back to the 1970s, initial claims are very low, despite the growth of nonfarm payrolls over the decades. They were lower only during the tight labor market of 2018 and 2019 and during the labor shortages coming out of the pandemic.

This shows that layoffs are really not a problem in this unusual labor market.

The labor market is showing the effects of overhiring during the pandemic by companies that have since then laid off people and hired people at the same time, which is what Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) have done, with the net effect that their headcount, after exploding for a few years, has been roughly flat - more in a moment.

And the labor market may be showing the effects of the crackdown on illegal immigration and some tightening up of legal immigration, which reduce the supply of labor and slow hiring.

The Challenger Job Cuts Report, also out this morning by outplacement firm Challenger, Gray & Christmas, tracks layoff announcements by US companies of their global workforce. So these are not actual layoffs, but announcements of potential future layoffs globally.

In November, layoff announcements fell to 71,321. The layoff announcements this year were concentrated in February (172,017), March (275,240), and October (153,074).

When Amazon announced in late October that it would lay off 30,000 people of its global workforce - with an undisclosed portion to take place in the US, and the rest globally - it pumped up the Challenger Jobs Cut report to 153,000, even though those layoffs hadn’t happened yet, and not all might happen, and even though only some of the layoffs will take place in the US, and even though Amazon was hiring through the other door.

October’s 153,000 was the highest since March, and that would not have made effective "the job market is cracking" headlines. But it was the highest October since October 2020, and that did trigger a round of the "job market is cracking" headlines.

Continued unemployment insurance claims, also released on Thursday by the Labor Department, track people who applied for unemployment insurance at least a week earlier and are still claiming unemployment insurance because they still haven’t found a job.

These “continued unemployment claims” fell in the latest week to 1.939 million, the second week in a row of declines, and are down by 29,000 from the recent high at the end of July.

The four-week average dipped for the second week in a row to 1.945 million.

Over the past four decades, it’s only during the tight labor market in 2018 and 2019 and in the years since Covid, particularly the period of the labor shortages in 2021 and 2022, that the level was lower - despite the much larger nonfarm payrolls.

It does mean that people remain on the unemployment insurance rolls a little longer than in 2022-2024 and in 2018-2019, but not nearly as long as they did in the decades before 2018.

So layoffs are very low, but once laid off, it takes people longer to find a job, as companies have slowed their hiring.

Alphabet and Amazon, along with other tech giants, exemplify this low-fire, low-hire labor market. After the massive overhiring during the pandemic, which was in part responsible for the labor shortages during that time, they started trimming some of the excess in some places, starting in 2022, while still adding in other places - and their global headcount has remained roughly stable since 2022, after exploding in prior years.

Alphabet, which discloses the headcount in its quarterly filings, reported 190,167 employees globally at the end of Q3, up from year-end 2024 and flat with year-end 2022.

Between 2016 and 2023, its headcount had tripled! During the overhiring binge in 2020-2023, its headcount had soared by 62%. These are huge increases for big, mature companies.

Amazon, which discloses global year-end headcount in its annual reports, had 1.56 million employees at year-end 2024, slightly higher than in the prior two years but below the peak in 2021 (1.61 million).

Though a tech giant, a big portion of Amazon’s employees are in brick-and-mortar retail (including Whole Foods), ecommerce retail, warehousing, and transportation around the world.

Overhiring: In the two years of 2020 and 2021, its headcount had doubled. From year-end 2015 through year-end 2021, its headcount had multiplied by seven! These are gigantic increases.

Since 2021, it has laid off people and hired people simultaneously - though it’s the layoff announcements that get into the news, a time-honored corporate strategy, eagerly spread by the crisis media, to cow restless employees.

The low-fire, low-hire labor market also shows up in employment data, where job creation has flattened out: Private-Sector Job Trends by Industry: From Job Destruction in “Information” to Job Creation in “Mining & Natural Resources”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Inditex Q3 2025 Earnings Analysis: Growth Rebound Seems To Be Priced In
data.description:
As I’ve covered in previous articles, Industria de Diseno Textil – Inditex (OTCPK:IDEXY) (OTCPK:IDEXF) is a company with strong fundamentals in the global retail sector but was facing some growth headwinds in the recent past. This was the main reason I was cautious about its stock some months ago, even though over the long term I think the company has a great business model that is not easy to replicate. Despite my cautious view, its shares have performed relatively well since my last article, beating the overall stock market by a small margin in recent months.

This was supported by better operating trends recently, reversing from a slowdown in previous quarters and boding well for business growth ahead. As the company has recently released its third quarter results related to the 2025 Fiscal Year [FY], which ends next January, I think it’s now a good time to analyze its most recent financial performance and update its investment case, to see if Inditex offers more value to long-term investors or not.

Q3 FY 2025 Earnings Analysis

During the third quarter of fiscal year 2025 (which goes from August to October), Inditex reported higher sales growth than in previous quarters as customers reacted positively to discounts and promotions at the end of the spring/summer collection. Its sales amounted to more than €9.8 billion in the quarter, up by 4.9% YoY, or +8.4% YoY at constant currencies. Forex has been a headwind for the company’s sales growth over the past few quarters and is expected to negatively impact its sales growth by about 4% in FY 2025, having a considerable impact on its top-line performance.

Due to its investments over the past couple of years in logistics and sourcing, Inditex was able to report gross margin growth of 6.2% YoY, being above its sales growth in the period. This is a very positive development, as the company has invested aggressively to expand its logistics during 2024-2025, while sales growth slowed down at the same time. This raised some concerns that Inditex’s logistics expansion plans could backfire, but higher gross margin is a good sign that Inditex made the right decision and is now able to move inventory faster to stores and at lower costs.

In Q2 FY 2025, Inditex’s gross margin was 62.2%, representing an increase of 80 basis points (bps) compared to the same quarter of last fiscal year, being an important contribution to earnings growth in the period.

Additionally, the company was also able to have good cost control, given that operating expenses increased by only 3% YoY to nearly €2.9 billion in the quarter, a very good outcome considering the inflationary environment and cost pressures globally. As revenue growth was stronger than cost growth and Inditex was also able to expand its gross margin, its business delivered positive operating leverage during the last quarter. This explains why its EBITDA increased by 8.9% YoY to almost €3.2 billion in the quarter and its net profit was above €1.8 billion (+9% YoY), being higher growth rates than sales growth in the period.

During the first nine months of FY 2025, Inditex’s revenues increased by only 2.7% YoY to €28.1 billion, and its net profit was up by 3.9% YoY to more than €4.6 billion, showing that operating momentum has picked up during the last quarter, which is a good sign that the company is progressing well on returning to stronger growth after a period of slowdown. This was largely driven by external factors, including soft consumer demand across its most important markets and currency headwinds, which seem also to be easing recently.

Its winter collection was also well received by customers, leading to Inditex’s sales growth being quite strong at 10.6% in November at constant currencies, significantly higher than in previous months. Considering that the Christmas period is usually strong for retail chains like Inditex, this bodes well for the company’s Q4 performance. Despite that, current consensus expects revenues in the next quarter to be around €11.7 billion, which represents annual growth of only 4.5% YoY, which seems to be conservative following the strong start to the quarter. Thus, these estimates may be revised upwards in the short term as analysts seem to be too conservative regarding sales growth in Q4.

Another factor that is positive for its sales growth in the short term is the company’s inventory levels, which were around €4.5 billion at the end of last September, representing about 34 days of sales. This is an acceptable level of inventory and reduces pressure on Inditex to do discounts and promotions to clear inventory in the coming months, assuming the Christmas season goes well regarding sales, which is also an important factor for the company to protect its business margins.

Regarding its expansion efforts, Inditex opened stores in 39 markets during the last quarter and reached more than 5,500 stores at the end of last October. The company is still targeting annual gross space growth of about 5%, which will be key to maintaining strong business growth ahead, together with higher penetration of online sales.

Regarding its balance sheet, Inditex generates significant cash flow and has a very strong financial position, as the company does not have almost any debt and had a cash position of €11.3 billion at the end of last quarter. While this could allow the company to distribute a large part of profits to shareholders, its strategy has been more focused on investing in business growth rather than returning capital to shareholders, explaining why its dividend payout ratio is somewhat conservative.

Indeed, while Inditex raised its interim dividend (ordinary plus special dividend) by about 9% to €0.84 per share last November, its dividend payout ratio is below 60%, which is a somewhat conservative level in my opinion for a company that is highly profitable and has a very strong financial position. Its total dividend during the year was €1.68 per share, which at its current share price leads to a dividend yield of about 3.1%. This is lower than when I last analyzed Inditex (it was yielding 3.5% at the time), as its share price has rebounded recently and its income appeal is now somewhat lower but still acceptable compared to other consumer stocks.

Going forward, there is some possibility that Inditex may decide to become more aggressive regarding its dividend growth, but most likely it should follow a similar path to earnings growth ahead. As free cash flow generation is expected to improve in fiscal year 2026 following two years of large investments in logistics, the company will have more financial flexibility to return capital to shareholders, most likely through special dividends, as the company historically has made minimal share buyback programs.

This happens because the company prioritizes cash flow generation back to business growth rather than returning capital to shareholders, seeing this as the best way to differentiate itself in the retail sector and remain competitive over the long term. Therefore, even though free cash flow generation should be stronger in the coming years compared to FY 2024 and 2025, I don’t think the company’s capital return strategy will change much in the near future.

Regarding its valuation, Inditex is currently trading at about 27x forward earnings, being somewhat higher than when I last analyzed it (23x forward earnings at the time). This higher valuation multiple is justified by the stock’s strong reaction to its most recent earnings, pushing its valuation above its ‘normal’ range of between 20-25x earnings. As can be seen in the next graph, its valuation is nowadays close to the top of its historical range, being clearly a sign that its shares aren’t undervalued right now.

Conclusion

Inditex is a company with a strong business model and fundamentals in the consumer sector, but its business growth showed some weakness in recent quarters, mainly because of cyclical factors, while a rebound was achieved in the last quarter and its sales performance for the beginning of Q4 is encouraging. While there is some uncertainty if this momentum keeps going over the next few quarters, the market reacted quite well to this growth pickup, pushing the valuation of its shares to relatively high levels. Because of that, even though Inditex is a great company and has a very strong business model, I maintain my ‘hold’ recommendation for the time being, as I see its shares already pricing much of its growth ahead.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 3 In A Row From The Fed, But More Cautious Moves Expected In 2026
data.description:
By James Knightley | Padhraic Garvey, CFA | Chris Turner

The Fed had appeared reluctant to cut

There is a recognition within the Federal Reserve that even after 150bp of cumulative interest rate cuts, monetary policy remains modestly restrictive. However, officials' relative position on the risks to the Fed’s dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment is becoming more dispersed.

Expectations have swung wildly in the build-up to the 10 December FOMC meeting. At the press conference that followed the 29 October rate cut decision, Fed Chair Jerome Powell suggested that a further reduction in December "is not a foregone conclusion, far from it." The minutes to that decision showed "many" members were "leaning" against a December rate cut, and there was a sense that a lack of official data ahead of the December meeting, caused by delayed collection due to the government shutdown, was not going to help change their minds. On 19 November, we got down to just 7bp of a 25bp December cut being priced.

The weaker jobs story now favours a third 25bp cut for the year

In terms of the hawks, several Fed members remain concerned that the potential for stickier, more elevated inflation from tariffs can’t be ignored, while higher insurance costs are also an issue. The economy is still growing, equity markets are at all-time highs, and unemployment remains very low, so to them, there doesn’t appear to be a pressing need to cut rates again.

However, the more dovish members of the Fed argue that the second part of their mandate, maximising employment, is looking more challenged. Over the past two years, more than 90% of all US jobs created have originated in the leisure and hospitality sector, government and private education and healthcare services, and these three sectors look vulnerable to government spending changes and weak consumer sentiment. Moreover, all other sectors combined – such as retail, business services, transport & logistics, technology, and financial services – have lost jobs over the previous five months, as you can see in the chart below. Private surveys on hiring and layoff intentions have been weak, and there have been high-profile job-loss announcements from the likes of Amazon, Target, Paramount, and UPS in recent weeks. The jobs mandate, therefore, argues for further rate cuts from the Fed.

At one point, it did appear that some of the relative doves, including Governor Chris Waller, were wavering on how quickly further rate cuts would come through. However, more robust language has been used in the past few weeks, and with New York Fed President John Williams seemingly breaking in favour of a rate cut, who is seen as more of a centrist, there is now a clear sense that momentum has swung back towards a rate cut. Weaker ADP jobs data, Challenger layoff numbers, and a downbeat assessment of the economy from the Fed’s own Beige Book offer further support for the rate-cut narrative, with 23 bp of a 25 bp cut now discounted. We agree and look for a 25bp rate cut, but it will be a split outcome with the prospect that we see four members vote for a no-change decision.

90% of US jobs created in just three sectors - jobs lost everywhere else

Monthly change (000s)

Fed to continue signaling 1 cut in 2026, but the risks are skewed to more action

The key question is what will the Fed signal for next year, given that we will be getting a new forecast update from them. Last time out, they indicated that just one rate cut in 2026 was their central case, in an environment where the economy expands at a 1.8% rate, unemployment holds around 4.4%, and core inflation ends the year above target at 2.6%. Given US unemployment is already 4.44%, this is subject to some upside risk due to the low-hire, low-fire economy, but we doubt the Fed will suddenly become more relaxed on the inflation narrative given the lack of timely data. As such, the most dovish they could possibly be is to put in a second rate cut for their 2026 forecast, but they will be reluctant.

But does this matter, given that we know the Federal Reserve's structure is changing? From May, the Fed will have a new Chair. Kevin Hassett, the current Director of the National Economic Council, is the hotly tipped favourite. He is an advocate for lower rates, and if President Trump is successful in forcing out Governor Lisa Cook, we could see the appointment of a replacement who is equally inclined to lower borrowing costs. This would mean that five of the seven members of the Board of Governors are Trump appointees. Furthermore, in February, all 12 regional Fed presidents are up for reappointment – candidates are nominated by regional Fed banks' management but can be vetoed by the Board of Governors. This creates the possibility of a revamped Fed that thinks very differently from the current committee.

We suspect the inflation backdrop will become more conducive for interest rate cuts in the coming months, thus giving the doves the justification for further action. While the tariff threat lingers, it is coming through more slowly and less forcibly than feared. This allows more time for disinflationary forces from lower energy prices, slowing housing rents and weaker wage growth to mitigate and, we believe, push inflation closer to 2% more quickly than the Fed is forecasting.

With the inflation backdrop looking less threatening but the jobs story becoming more fragile, we see the Fed cutting rates twice in 2026, with moves in March and in June. In combination with a bit more fiscal support in 2026, this should create the required platform for growth. Nonetheless, the potential for a more dovish FOMC tilts the risks toward additional rate cuts later in the year.

There is still a containment job to be done in the face of liquidity tightness

At the last FOMC meeting, the Fed announced a freezing of its balance sheet. The roll-off of the MBS portfolio continues but is broadly offset by the buying of T-bills. That should then stabilise bank reserves at or near current levels. A dominant rationale for the balance sheet freeze was the prior rise in the effective funds rate (relative to the other fixed policy rates). The spread from the funds rate to the rate on excess reserves (IOER) was 7bp. That had narrowed to 3bp. Not a great look, reflecting liquidity pressures.

Fast forward to today, and the effective funds rate (3.89%) has drifted up even tighter to the IOER (3.90%); the spread is now just 1bp. It’s reflective of continued tightness in conditions and elevated market repo rates. The funds rate can technically go above the IOER, but would likely not go too far above, as there is then an arbitrage back into the IOER bucket from the funds rate bucket. Still, all of this is somewhat sub-optimal, as the effective funds rate is then getting closer to the ceiling on the funds rate range (currently set at 4%).

To address all of this, it’s quite possible that the Fed decides to buy more bills than required from the MBS roll-off, thus acting to increase bank reserves. We don’t think the Fed needs to do anything dramatic here, maybe only to announce that they have that flexibility. In fact, repo has tamed in the past day or so (post the month-end turn), which could in fact pull the funds rate back down a tad before the FOMC meeting. These subtle policy changes won’t make the headlines, but they are important, as proper market functioning is a key Fed responsibility.

FOMC is a positive event risk for the dollar

For a market that seems to have so casually priced in an extension of the Fed easing cycle into next year, the December FOMC’s reality check with Fed thinking could pose some upside risks for the dollar. Here, the Fed could easily, after three back-to-back cuts, cite the fact that the policy rate is much closer to neutral, requiring some kind of pause in the rate-cutting cycle or at least a hint at cutting rates at alternate policy meetings.

As the October FOMC meeting showed, press conferences can prove quite dangerous for dollar bears, and it is hard to see Powell delivering a more dovish-than-expected update given the dissent within the Fed. However, that does not mean the dollar has to bounce too far. Market pricing, compared to our own views, is reasonably conservative at pricing only an additional 14bp of cuts by the time of the March meeting (ING -25bp) and an additional 33bp of cuts by the June meeting (-50bp).

There is an outside risk of EUR/USD briefly correcting back under 1.16 and USD/JPY to 156/157 on this Fed event risk. But our bias remains that year-end seasonal dollar weakness, perhaps some soft US jobs data on December 16th, and a BoJ rate hike on December 19th can see EUR/USD and USD/JPY head to the 1.18 and 152 areas, respectively, by year-end.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Market Bubble? Buy These Defensive Dividends Instead, Earn +7.5% Income
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Black Friday 2025 has come and gone, and the series of extended Cyber Monday deals continues to clog our inboxes. Retailers love to start the party early and keep the party going with one “limited-time” sale after another. This will continue through Christmas and the New Year; after all, the holiday season is the best time to be a shopper, right? Discounts, bundles, doorbusters—everywhere you look, it’s hard to control the urge to buy something.

I am fascinated by the concept of “sale.” It is such a magical word; something costs less today than it did yesterday, and we love it. We brag about finding deals. When something we want is selling for 20% off, it's the best thing ever.

Imagine if a retailer put up a sign, “Black Friday: Pay 20% more today only!” I guarantee you, no one would show up. Not a single person would camp all night in the cold to pay a premium on purpose.

And yet. . . in the stock market, that’s exactly what happens every day, every year.

Investors routinely avoid stocks just because their prices are down. At the same time, they have no problem rushing into overpriced assets because they’re going “to the moon.” I’ve lost count of how many times I’ve seen comments like “I hate seeing red in my portfolio.”

I agree; no one enjoys watching something they own drop in value. But markets don’t travel in straight lines, and it is not like you are done being a buyer. Personally, I look forward to buying more next year than I did this year, and lower prices are a good thing for buyers. Paying higher prices is not the solution to avoiding the sight of red in your portfolio. Even the biggest winners make repeated stops at FUDville, Paintown, and Anxiety Junction—places where those diamond hands tend to crack under pressure.

At High Dividend Opportunities, rather than chasing hype, we look for quality securities trading at discount prices. We like our stuff, and our stocks at bargain prices, and we will now discuss our top picks.

Pick #1: UTF – Yield 7.8%

“Price is what you pay, value is what you get” – Warren Buffett

Publicly traded funds are required to transparently report their Net Asset Value, which is a metric reflective of the value of all their assets, minus all liabilities, on a per share basis. The chart below clearly shows the value you are getting, for the price you pay.

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF) is a CEF (closed-end fund) composed of exactly what its name says—infrastructure, comprising electric utility, midstream corporations, gas distribution, freight rail, airport, and toll road companies. Source

In recent times, the fund has been heavily allocated to energy and utilities, taking advantage of the surging demand for reliable power for data center expansion. About 55% of UTF’s holdings are US-based companies, while companies from Canada, the U.K., Japan, Australia, and Mexico represent ~26%. The CEF’s top ten holdings that comprise a third of the assets are some of the largest utility/energy companies in North America.

UTF recently completed a tender offer, which resulted in the issuance of 14.9 million shares and $353 million in proceeds, implying a 15% dilution for shareholders. This has led to a sell-off in the stock, creating a rare discount that we haven’t seen in years.

With $3.5 billion in net assets (including leverage), UTF is already a large fund with a reliable track record of over 20 years. We believe that more capital will lead to a larger portfolio, which will translate to larger earnings from industries that form the building blocks of the economy, and experience inelastic demand against market pressures. UTF’s leverage is linked with the SOFR (floating rate), and the fund reported a weighted average interest rate of 5.2% for the first six months of 2025. We expect this leverage structure to experience a tailwind from declining interest rates.

UTF pays $0.1550/share, implying a 7.8% yield. There was $184.2 million in unrealized appreciation as of June 30, 2025, indicating coverage for a whole year of distributions (less on a forward-looking basis, since UTF will have 15% more shares outstanding).

In the years ahead, nations will be consuming more power than they do today. The focus on AI increases the pace of that consumption, and if you look around, VCs aren’t rushing to spin up boring, highly-regulated, capital-intensive, slow-growth utility companies. However, without them, there is no AI race or advancement. It will be like having the best smartphone in the world—with no chargers. Investors globally are investing in existing utility companies, placing the sector at the higher side of the valuation curve over the past two decades.

With UTF, you can grab a slice of this necessary sector at a 6% discount.

Pick #2: GMRE Preferreds – Up to 8% Yields

Global Medical REIT Inc. (GMRE) is a net-lease medical REIT (Real Estate Investment Trust) that operated 191 properties, leased out to 315 tenants as of September 30, 2025. The company reported an occupancy level of 95.2% (a sequential improvement) at the end of the third quarter, and a weighted-average lease term of 5.3 years. GMRE was previously impacted by the Steward Health Care bankruptcy, since Steward occupied its healthcare facility in Beaumont, Texas. The REIT has since successfully re-leased the property to an affiliate of Christus Health, through a 15-year triple-net lease.

In January, the REIT announced a succession plan for its CEO position, and in June, finalized the appointment of Mr. Mark Decker Jr. as President and CEO. A few weeks prior to this confirmation, the REIT cut its dividend by 28% in an effort that management termed “rightsizing our dividend” to improve the REIT’s financial flexibility and balance sheet health. In September, GMRE completed a 1:5 reverse split of its common stock.

YTD, GMRE spent $69.6 million on property acquisitions, at a weighted average cap rate of 8.7%, while disposing of five assets to raise $13.6 million in gross proceeds. Three of these properties were occupied, and had a cap rate of 7%. During the third quarter, the REIT completed several refinancing arrangements, pushing out 2026 maturities to 2029 and beyond. As such, the REIT ended Q3 with $712.8 million in gross debt, with 70% of it carrying fixed interest rates, and a weighted-average cost of all borrowing at a reasonable 3.96%, and a weighted-average duration of 4.4 years (up from 1.6 years at the end of Q2).

During the Q3 earnings announcement, management narrowed their previous full-year 2025 AFFO per share to between $4.45 and $4.65, placing the REIT's new annual dividend at a 65% payout ratio.

GMRE delivered a strong quarter, with a positive outlook ahead, targeting a 96% occupancy by the end of the fiscal year. The REIT’s common stock dividend reduction does not alter the thesis for the perpetual preferreds. Interestingly, GMRE-A had been trading post-call date for over three years (despite rising interest rates), and now trades at a discount since the REIT recently issued GMRE-B at an 8% coupon. GMRE-A currently trades post its call date at a slight discount to par, offering a 7.8% yield and ~4% upside. GMRE-B trades at a slight premium, presenting an 8% yield for at least another five years, before it becomes callable.

  • 7.50% Series A, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GMRE.PR.A) - Yield 7.8%
  • 8% Series B, Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Stock (GMRE.PR.B) - Yield 8%

GMRE spent $4.3 million on preferred dividends during the first nine months of 2025, against $48.8 million in AFFO generated during this period. Overall, we see excellent coverage for the preferred dividends, allowing shareholders to collect higher yields from a sound operator of medical properties.

Conclusion

In bull markets, investors tend to chase future potential and are often willing to pay several times the “potential” future cash flows. At High Dividend Opportunities, we prefer companies that generate cold hard cash today, that they share with investors. As income investors, we’re well-positioned to remain buyers through market gyrations. Our next paycheck is always right around the corner, built from steady dividends from our holdings. This lets us accumulate more assets at better prices. That is how patience turns volatility into wealth!

data.title: Rocket Lab Is Taking Off Again: Why I Like The $40 Level (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Rocket Lab (RKLB) certainly didn’t get any investor love over the past month. The stock has been free-falling from its high of $73 back in October, but November was something else.

After reporting earnings in early November, the stock shot up 10%, and since then (until 2 days ago), the stock was in the red, hitting as low as $37.5 back in late November. Momentum is picking back up, and we saw the stock up 6% and 10% this Wednesday and Thursday, respectively. The stock is now up around 18% in just 5 days.

With technicals and the long-term fundamentals finally aligning again, I think we’re starting to see Rocket Lab stock stabilize at a level where buyers can step back in. I’m hence upgrading the stock to a strong buy and think investors still have the chance to get in at the $40 level before the next leg up. With RSI going back to the 52 level, in bullish territory, and volume ramping aggressively on the last two green candles, the stock is finally showing signs of a genuine momentum reversal rather than just a dead-cat bounce. The stock reclaimed the $45 level, so if it holds that level in the coming trading days, the next test will be the EMA50 at around $50. A breakout above that sets up a clean move to the $53.50 level, which is where the heavier resistance sits.

So, what pushed Rocket Lab +10% higher on Thursday?

Rocket Lab took over the headlines on Thursday as the company was recognized as New Zealand’s Company of the Year. The award highlights Rocket Lab’s growing role as a catalyst for the next wave of Kiwi innovation, and reinforces the company’s positioning as one of the most important space players outside the U.S.

But dare I say, since sentiment was so beaten down last month, I think this is the breather investors needed. Peter Beck took to the stage and said, “We had a great 2025, but man, 2026 is going to be way, way bigger.” I’m a firm believer that a company is only as strong as the management behind it, and Beck continues to deliver the conviction and clarity long-term investors want to hear.

Rocket Lab is also getting renewed attention on the Street. Cantor Fitzgerald reaffirmed its Overweight rating and kept its $72 price target, highlighting the company’s strong Q3 beat and $1.1B backlog. The analyst even framed the recent pullback as a buying opportunity heading into several key catalysts, reinforcing the idea that the market may be starting to reprice RKLB’s long-term trajectory.

All in all, sentiment is finally starting to shift back in Rocket Lab’s favor.

Investment thesis

After a brutal November that flushed out weak hands, this is the reset we needed. If Rocket Lab delivers on its 2026 roadmap as Beck is signaling, this could mark the beginning of a broader re-rating.

My thesis on the stock hasn’t changed pre-pullback, but I am more wary of another Neutron delay. Regardless, I think the Neutron story remains intact in 2026, and it remains the mold of Rocket Lab’s magic and a catalyst that will significantly unlock a new phase of valuation for the company. If Rocket Lab can demonstrate meaningful progress on Neutron’s development and maintain cadence toward its first flight milestones, the market will start pricing in the long-term revenue streams tied to higher-value missions.

Risks going forward

If the company fails to scale Neutron successfully and prove it can deliver cost-efficient launches while simultaneously growing the Space Systems segment, I think the long-term thesis starts to break down. But if management delivers, even incrementally, 2026 could be the year Neutron transitions from a narrative to a tangible economic engine. All I’m saying is, Neutron’s commercialization can’t slip or fail to mature into a competitive, economically viable launch vehicle, as a big part of the current valuation is riding on it. At first glance, forward EV/Sales of 43.15x, versus a sector median of 2.18x, and a forward Price/Sales of 44.00x, compared with 1.78x for the industry, screams expensive. Even from a balance-sheet perspective, Rocket Lab’s forward price/book stands at 21.08x, dramatically higher than the sector median of 3.33x. Still, at current levels, I continue to see Rocket Lab as one of the most compelling asymmetric bets in the space sector heading into 2026.

I’m telling investors to buy the November dip back to the $40 level.

data.title: Value Line Is Shareholder-Friendly, But It Faces Serious Headwinds
data.description:
Value Line (VALU) is a financial information and services company. Some of its informational products have existed for more than 100 years. The company, in its modern form, was founded back in 1982. It is primarily known for products such as The Value Line Investment Survey, which provide investors with a great deal of financial information about publicly-traded companies.

VALU stock has had an uneven run in the public markets. The stock didn't do much in the 1990s, despite the dot-com stock boom, as the company's more fundamental-focused research didn't fit the moment's speculative enthusiasm.

Shares plunged in the late 2000s as the stock market tanked, thus leading to falling investor interest in financial information in general, along with the fact that competing internet-based research was improving in both availability and quality.

After falling to the single digits, however, VALU stock enjoyed a remarkable turnaround. Shares briefly hit the $100 mark a few years ago. While the stock has cooled off since then, it still remains far above where it was trading 10 or 15 years ago:

This might perplex some investors. In an age of digital research and newsletters, who is still subscribing to Value Line services? Or for another example, it probably seems anachronistic to go to a public library to use their copy of the Value Line manual to look up information on a company. So how has Value Line adapted to the changing times, and is it a good investment today?

Value Line's Business Model

Value Line is most-known for its publications and periodicals business. The company describes its offerings in this segment as falling within the following four categories:

  • Comprehensive reference periodical publications
  • Targeted, niche periodical newsletters
  • Investment analysis software
  • Current and historical financial databases

The print versions of these services have seen their revenues gradually erode over time. That said, some of these offerings may be more resilient than expected, such as the sales of comprehensive reference publications to libraries and institutions.

Value Line has been able to transition a fair bit of its publications revenue to digital subscriptions and services. A common use case there, for example, would be local libraries that allow their patrons to access Value Line's subscription services through an online library portal. Value Line also has investment research software of its own, along with selling niche newsletters to individual investors over the internet.

While the company has made admirable strides to try to protect the business from technological disruption and changing consumer behavior, it will take strong management performance to keep that business' revenues steady, let alone growing.

And because, historically, the periodicals division was the largest business operation at Value Line, its eroding outlook has caused overall revenues to flatline.

There was a huge drop in revenues in the late 2000s. Things settled out from there for the next decade and even grew slightly in the early 2020s, but revenues have resumed their decline and are now near fresh lows:

Keep in mind that this is in nominal dollars as well; if you adjust for inflation, this picture would look even worse.

So, how has VALU stock held up in the face of such a grim top-line revenue profile?

It has two other ways of generating value for shareholders.

The first is that it has a net cash balance sheet, and it invests the cash in various marketable securities. These investments have generated decent returns over time, including a stronger run of performance in recent years. Market returns can be fickle, and it wouldn't be surprising if investment gains diminished or outright turned to losses in a recession/bear market scenario. There's also the fact that we've had a higher interest rate environment in recent years, which allows for generating more returns on cash and fixed income holdings. This could reverse course in coming years, especially if central banks aggressively cut their rates.

The real driver of investor excitement around Value Line, especially in 2021-2022, was with its asset management. Since 2010, the EULAV Asset Management Trust ("EAM") has managed Value Line-branded funds. Value Line receives a chunk of certain EAM revenues and residual profits but does not have any voting interest in selecting EAM's leadership.

This operation's assets under management surged 35% in 2024, driven by both strong inflows and upbeat market performance. However, that came after a 2023 in which EAM's assets under management fell 8% versus the prior year. All this to say that while recent results have been strong, we should expect to see volatility rather than steady growth for this unit.

Value Line also licenses its name and indexes out to third parties that operate ETFs based on that intellectual property; these revenues are labeled as copyright fees. For example, the First Trust Value Line® Dividend Index Fund ETF (FVD) has more than $8 billion under management, and Value Line receives some economic compensation for the use of its brand in the marketing of that fund. Value Line's copyright fee revenues have been stagnant over the past couple of years, and so I would not model in any significant growth from this going forward unless a Value Line-branded product really takes off.

How Value Line Has Maintained Shareholder Value

Given this fairly uninspiring recent history and growth prospects, you might think that VALU stock would be trading much lower. After all, the stock traded down to the single digits in the 2010s, and revenues are lower now than they were then. So, why is the stock at $38?

First of all, while revenues are down, earnings per share surged since the early 2010 doldrums:

This is likely due to the company's business mix. The periodicals business requires significant overhead, such as hiring finance professionals to research and write the publications, in addition to the printing and distribution of physical books and newsletters.

As more of Value Line's profit mix comes from copyright fees, revenues from EAM, and gains on investments, the firm's margins should increase. While it is unfortunate that the firm's revenues keep declining, at least the revenues have been healthier within higher-margin parts of the business.

The other key factor is that Value Line has had a steady share buyback running, reducing its share count from 10 million in the early 2010s to 9.4 million now.

Admittedly, a six percent reduction in share count over a more than decade-long span doesn't sound like much. However, Arnold Bernhard & Co., Inc. ("ABC"), the predecessor of Value Line, retains ownership of more than 91% of all outstanding VALU stock. This means that there is a public tradable float of less than one million shares of Value Line stock today. Against that backdrop, the share buyback is eating up a large chunk of the freely-trading shares of the company. I believe this has helped keep VALU stock at a higher valuation than you'd otherwise see for a firm with such structurally challenged business prospects.

Specifically, Value Line is trading at about 16x trailing earnings. And, over time, I'd expect the firm's EPS to be at best steady and perhaps decline in the years to come, depending on the rate at which publication revenues continue to trail off.

The firm has a shareholder-friendly capital return policy. It pays out more than half of earnings as dividends and has increased the dividend for 11 consecutive years. However, as I believe EPS growth will be minimal in coming years, I would not bank on rapid dividend growth either. And the starting 3.4% dividend yield isn't high enough to be especially attractive in its own right given the weak growth prospects of the business.

Value Line Stock Bottom Line

Value Line is still solidly profitable, and its revenues had been steady in nominal terms, at least until recently. This isn't a growing business any longer, but it hasn't become obsolete nearly as fast as the skeptics may have predicted.

Furthermore, the company's sound capital allocation decisions have allowed it to generate some shareholder returns despite the serious structural challenges that the business faces.

Given the small share count and the company's willingness to repurchase shares, it's not impossible to imagine shares jumping at some point. See 2022, for example, when Value Line shares skyrocketed, seemingly around optimism tied to the asset management business. There could be a profitable trade to be had.

In my opinion, however, there's not enough here to try to take a contrarian bullish position. People still want investment advice, but more frequently folks are turning to other venues, rather than Value Line's manuals, to get that information about stocks. The asset management arm diversifies the business to a degree, but once again, I doubt Value Line will see significant inflows compared to funds that have more effective marketing and branding with younger investors.

It's a credit to management that they've managed to keep the financial results stable amid so many headwinds to the core business model. That said, it will be akin to running uphill just to try to keep earnings flat, let alone increase them in the years to come. And neither the valuation nor the dividend yield is compelling enough to justify deploying capital here over other alternatives. VALU stock is not especially expensive, and management has behaved in a shareholder-friendly manner. So, I'm not at an outright sell on the stock, but I see little reason to buy it, either.

data.title: Arthur J. Gallagher & Co.: Major Earnings Growth Ahead
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

It's often said that it's better to have survival tools and not need them than to need survival tools and not have them. The extra weight of having those tools that might save your life in an emergency is worth carrying.

When it comes to our day-to-day lives, we think very little of the auto insurance that we have on our vehicles, outside the burden of having to pay for it and the requirements to have it. We find very little value in that insurance until we need it. The moment that a car accident occurs, suddenly you're thankful or appreciative to have this coverage. For the rest of us, who are not experiencing an accident at that moment, our auto insurance is nothing more than an expense on our budget.

However, the financial ramifications of not having insurance in a moment of need can be severe and significant.

As a dividend investor, I am always keen to find opportunities to collect wonderful dividends and strong total returns from companies that are not at the forefront of investors' or consumers' minds. While I think that owning shares of Coca-Cola (KO) or Pepsi (PEP) can be exciting and rewarding, I specifically love to target the companies that exist in the Ethereal Plane. What I mean by this is companies that exist in a realm outside the average consciousness of investors or consumers. Many of these companies thrive and benefit from this lack of public knowledge and exposure.

If you look over many of my recent articles on Seeking Alpha, you'll see that we have covered companies that are not well-known to the Seeking Alpha public, or even the public in general, and have low followership. Why would we write articles about them and share them with everyone? Because we know there is tremendous value in investing in companies that others aren't actively following. These companies can be undervalued because of the lack of public knowledge about them, providing you with opportunities to collect wonderful, growing dividends and see strong total returns over the long run.

Why rush in and push against the crowd when you can walk in leisurely to an investment and enjoy the total returns? It's why the Dividend King's Model Portfolio is performing so strongly.

Today I want to take a look at one of these companies that operate outside the standard knowledge of investors in the public.

Let's dive in!

The Wizard Of Insurance Brokerage Roll-Ups

Arthur J. Gallagher & Co. (AJG) operates in 130 countries around the world and generated nearly $13 billion in total revenue over the past four quarters. AJG is a major insurance brokerage firm.

The company is divided into two segments: Brokerage and Risk Management.

The former advises and consults on insurance programs and places insurance for clients. This made up 88% of AJG's total revenue (76% of Commission & Fee revenue is commission-based, and the other 24% is fee-based). This included retail property and casualty insurance (51% of Brokerage segment revenue), retail benefits (23%), wholesale (14%), and reinsurance (12%).

The Risk Management segment adjusts claims and helps companies and carriers reduce their losses across Workers' compensation, liability, managed care, auto, and property insurance. This contributed the remaining 12% of revenue.

The big picture for AJG is encouraging. The company is a way to play the growing global insurance brokerage market. As Kody outlined a couple of weeks ago with my Marsh & McLennan (MMC) listicle, AJG stands to be another beneficiary of the growing demand for insurance. Mordor Intelligence anticipates that the global insurance brokerage industry will grow by 9.5% each year, from $328.5 billion in 2025 to $480.7 billion by 2030.

What's more, AJG has demonstrated itself to be a frequent M&A participant. Its $13.8 billion acquisition of AssuredPartners, completed in August, is the latest example. This deal further expands its presence in the middle-market P&C and employee benefits areas. Including synergies, AJG believes it can deliver double-digit adjusted EPS accretion from the transaction.

These elements are why I agree with the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for adjusted EPS to rise by 17.4% annually through 2027, off a 2024 base of $10.09.

The 1% dividend yield is modest. However, AJG's payout ratio is set to be in the mid-20% range for 2025. This provides a runway for the company to lengthen its 14-year dividend growth streak.

At the current $249 share price, the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 18.9 (11% weight for 2025 and 89% for 2026). That's 20% less than the 10-year average P/E ratio and my fair value P/E ratio of 23.5 ($309 fair value per share estimate).

If AJG lives up to the growth consensus and reverts to fair value, a 28% surge by the end of 2026 would be justified by the fundamentals. Through 2030, the stock appears poised to return 16% annually.

AJG's pertinent risks include merger integration risks and talent attraction/retention risks. If the company can't recognize the anticipated synergies from its AssuredPartners acquisition (or any future acquisitions), that could hurt financial results. To maintain its success, AJG must also attract and retain the brightest minds in the industry. If it can't do so, the company could lose market share to competitors.

Conclusion

When it comes to insurance, AJG has done a fantastic job of being a behind-the-scenes success. They do so by connecting clients to insurance solutions and are also involved in the reinsurance industry, offering insurance to those who give insurance, reducing their overall risk. AJG has a strong history of growing its dividends, and its payout ratio remains exceptionally low, leaving room for continued dividend growth going forward. Recently, AJG has accelerated its dividend growth, a trend I'd like to see continue.

A dividend portfolio designed for high total returns and high dividend growth can be fueled by companies growing their dividend rapidly, like AJG. When combined with wonderful opportunities that have a higher yield and low dividend growth, you can get a life-changing combination. These are the kinds of investments that I put into my personal portfolio to see market-beating returns while collecting market-beating yields at the same time. Each individual piece of a portfolio is like a brick that makes up the foundation of a home. Each brick itself may be inconsequential, but together they create a firm foundation for financial success.

What does your financial future look like? What are your financial goals? Is your portfolio supporting those goals or operating on its own?

It's important to have a holistic financial perspective of your life, and that means your portfolio needs to play its part, every single piece of it.

Plant your seeds. Watch them grow. Enjoy a bountiful harvest.
